info.topictitle: D4.1.9 Concept of the gene pool (HL)

markdown:
## Gene Pools Are the Foundation of Evolution


<definition term="Gene pool">The total collection of all alleles present in a population.</definition>

1. Changes in the gene pool drive the **adaptations** and **diversity** observed in species.

<callout type="hint">Changes in allele frequencies within this pool drive the **adaptations **and **diversity **we see in nature.</callout>

## Gene Pools Constantly Change Through Key Processes


The **gene pool** evolves as **allele frequencies** shift due to the following mechanisms:

1. **Mutations Introduce New Alleles: **Random mutations create **new genetic variations**, expanding the gene pool.
2. **Sexual Reproduction Shuffles Alleles: **By combining genetic material from two parents, sexual reproduction produces **unique allele combinations**.
3. **Natural Selection Favors the Fittest Alleles: **Traits that improve **survival** or **reproductive success** become more common in the gene pool.
4. **Genetic Drift Creates Random Changes: **In **small populations**, chance events cause **random fluctuations** in allele frequencies.

<callout type="example">Birds with **stronger wings** escape predators more easily, increasing their contribution to the gene pool.</callout>

## Genetic Equilibrium Is Rare in Nature


1. When all individuals in a population have an **equal chance** of contributing to the gene pool, the population is in **genetic equilibrium**.

<callout type="note">* This is a theoretical concept described by the **Hardy-Weinberg principle**, which assumes no evolution is occurring.
* See D4.13 for more (HL only).</callout>

<callout type="example">A tree population with a diverse gene pool may include alleles for **drought resistance**, helping some trees survive during dry spells.</callout>

## The Peppered Moth Shows How Gene Pools Evolve


1. Environmental changes during the Industrial Revolution caused a shift in the gene pool of the **peppered moth** population:
  1. **Before industrial pollution:** The **light form** of the moth was common because it blended with lichen-covered trees.
  2. **After industrial pollution:** The **dark form** became more prevalent, as it was better camouflaged against soot-covered bark.
2. **Outcome:** The gene pool shifted, increasing the frequency of the **dark allele**, a clear example of **natural selection** in action.

![Peppered moth as an example of shifting gene pool due to environmental factors](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/8ed156cb67c6038146a2fb9a29694989c58ef908-4000x4092.png)

<callout type="hint">To understand evolution, always consider how **selection pressures** and genetic diversity shape the gene pool over time.</callout>
